summary: Justin Brennan teaches critical mistakes to avoid when acquiring multifamily properties, emphasizing the importance of location over paper returns, proper due diligence, and scaling gradually rather than attempting zero-to-hero deals. He stresses managing key vendors (PMs, renovation teams) closely and avoiding undercapitalization to protect investor capital.
takeaways: Never sacrifice location for high cap rates on paper—location determines 99.9% of rental bumps and resale value
Don't chase paper returns; strong proformas can hide weak fundamentals in bad locations
Start with 10-15 or 20-30 unit deals before scaling up, not zero-to-hero 100+ unit deals on your first transaction
Over-capitalize deals slightly rather than under-capitalize and trigger capital calls to investors
Property managers typically need lots of properties to survive, so actively manage the managers and stay on them
Test renovation teams on a couple units first; never pay full contract upfront, use max 25% deposit
Do detailed due diligence on every deal—the Texas 225-unit example lost millions due to missed pipe/drainage issues from skipped checklist items

skill_candidates.change: Implement detailed due diligence checklist on every acquisition before closing
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin's Texas 225-unit case study where first-time syndicator lost millions by skipping due diligence on drainage/pipe failures; he emphasizes 'I can't stress this enough' and contrasts smaller early deals that taught him lessons with the catastrophic losses from skipped checklists

skill_candidates.change: Evaluate location quality as primary driver (99.9%) rather than optimizing for highest paper cap rate
skill_candidates.evidence: Repeated emphasis: 'Never, ever, ever sacrifice location' and 'Don't chase paper returns'; location provides 'safety net' during market changes and recessions where weak tertiary markets fail

skill_candidates.change: Start with 10-15 or 20-30 unit deals and self-manage portions before scaling to 100+ units
skill_candidates.evidence: Direct warning against zero-to-hero: 'don't just go zero to hero, guy...Start with kind of your smaller deals. You're going to learn a lot. Bigger size, learn, boom, then you go'; contrasts with the failed syndicator who went straight to 225 units with no experience

title: Lesson 1: Avoiding Big Mistakes
